preFix.title: Ragwort and rush control in a pasture
preFix.subtitle: The legal position on ragwort, and practical approaches to controlling rush on waterlogged land.
preFix.excerpt: Ragwort is subject to the Ragwort Control Act 2003 and the Code of Practice on how to prevent the spread of ragwort, which places a duty on landowners to control it near land used by horses or livestock. Rush indicates waterlogged or compacted soil and will return unless the drainage problem is addressed. This tutorial covers identification, control methods, and when to call in professional help.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice on how to prevent the spread of ragwort (2004). British Horse Society, ragwort poisoning factsheet. AHDB, rush control in pasture guidance.

content.text: Under the Ragwort Control Act 2003, enforcement notices can be issued where

poses a risk to grazing animals or adjacent land. The duty to control it arises when there is grazing livestock or horse-keeping land within spreading distance. Walk your fields in April and May when the rosettes are visible and easier to pull.

in spring, before the stem bolts. Pull the whole root; a broken root will regrow. Use a fork or purpose-made ragwort puller on heavy ground. Wear gloves; the alkaloids are absorbed through skin with repeated contact. Remove pulled plants from the field immediately; wilted ragwort is more attractive to animals than fresh. Bag and dispose of it in your domestic waste bin or by burning in a safe area. Do not compost it.

Where ragwort is dense over a large area, herbicide is more practical than hand-pulling. Aminopyralid products (e.g., Forefront T) are effective at the rosette stage. These are professional-use herbicides requiring PA1 and PA6 certificates. If you are not certificated, hire a contractor. Do not graze the treated area for the withdrawal period on the product label. Sprayed ragwort must still be removed from the field after it dies, as dead plants remain toxic.

in a pasture is a symptom of wet or compacted soil. Topping rush with a tractor-mounted mower in late spring reduces the seed bank but will not eliminate an established stand. Improvements in drainage (whether through mole draining, slit drainage, or clearing blocked ditches) are the only long-term control. On a small scale, targeted scraping and reseeding of rush-dominated patches with grass seed after drainage work can reclaim productive area.

Never make hay from a field containing ragwort. Ragwort is destroyed in the drying and baling process but the alkaloids remain active. Horses and cattle will eat ragwort in hay when they would avoid it fresh. Buy hay from known sources and inspect bales for dried ragwort stems before feeding.
